title: The Blind Curator: How a Biased Judge Silently Disables Skill Retirement in Self-Evolving Agents
summary: A self-evolving agent retires its bad skills by watching them fail, so what happens when the judge cannot see the failures? Skill retirement is the structural constraint that keeps a growing library from drifting below the no-skill baseline, but its guarantee assumes an unbiased reward, which is false for the LLM judges that reference-free tasks force upon us. We show that a biased judge does not merely add noise; it \emph{silently switches off the curator}. We make this precise with a corrupted-reward analysis and, isolating the causal channel by injecting corruption on top of a deterministic reward, a behavioral study on a reference-free report-writing testbed with a code-generation cross-check. Symmetric noise leaves retirement intact, but \emph{false-pass} bias (failures slipping through as passes) disables contribution-based retirement past a sharp threshold that no amount of data can cross. Separating genuine retirement from cap-eviction churn shows this \emph{mechanism} failure is universal, holding across domains and failure rates and sparing only near-zero-false-pass, verifier-like graders. The downstream \emph{outcome}, though, is regime-dependent: eval quality degrades only where the same corruption also starves skill synthesis, and otherwise holds steady, so the disabled curator is \emph{silent}, surfacing in no aggregate metric. The contribution is a behavioral safety result, not a performance one. A cheap defect-injection audit then tells an operator, before deployment, which side of the threshold their judge occupies.

processed.summary:
- Investigates the **Skill Retirement** mechanism in **Self-evolving Agents**, which is designed to prune failing skills to maintain a high-quality library.
- Identifies that **LLM Judges** used for reward signals suffer from **false-pass bias**, which silently disables the agent's ability to curate its skill library.
- Demonstrates that while symmetric noise is manageable, directional bias creates a **mechanism failure** where bad skills are never retired, regardless of data volume.
- Proposes a **defect-injection audit** as a safety tool for developers to detect if their automated judge will cause library degradation before deployment.
key_results: False-pass bias (failures slipping through as passes) is the specific driver that disables skill retirement.
The failure of the curator mechanism is 'silent' because it often does not show up in aggregate performance metrics.
There exists a sharp threshold of bias beyond which skill retirement becomes mathematically impossible.
Only 'verifier-like' graders with near-zero false-pass rates are immune to this structural degradation.
The study separates genuine retirement from 'cap-eviction churn' to prove the universality of this failure across domains.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers reveal that biased LLM judges prevent self-evolving agents from retiring bad skills, causing silent library degradation.

title: RLVP: Penalize the Path, Reward the Outcome
summary:
Agents acting on our behalf in the real world (e.g. placing phone calls) must learn online from costly, often irreversible interactions rather than cheap simulator steps. Two things follow. First, deployability depends on the path, not only the outcome. An agent must respect outcome-neutral constraints such as not repeatedly calling an unresponsive user, respecting business hours, or completing required authentication constraints that outcome-based rewards cannot express, since violating them frequently improves apparent success. Second, because each interaction is expensive, the agent must learn efficiently from very few examples.
  Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) is blind to both challenges: it optimizes solely on the outcome and wastes expensive rollouts on all-fail groups where group-relative advantage collapses to zero. Attempts to densify supervision by rewarding progress target the hard-to-verify direction. In contrast, real agentic environments can cheaply detect bad moves. Since group-relative advantage is equivalent to within-group variance, a dense signal helps only when it supplies variance the outcome lacks. A verifiable penalty on the path meets this condition reliably, while a progress potential helps only where partial progress is reachable. The resulting recipe "penalize the path, reward the outcome" achieves high task success with near-zero violations, where outcome-only training violates constraints on nearly every episode. We provide four design rules for effective penalties, including avoidance of the inaction trap that arises when a penalty is used in isolation.

processed.summary:
• Proposes **RLVP (Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Penalties)**, a framework designed for agents operating in real-world environments where interactions are costly and irreversible.
• Targets the limitations of **RLVR (Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards)**, which often fails to respect outcome-neutral constraints like business hours or authentication requirements.
• Implements a **dense signal architecture** that uses path-based penalties to reduce within-group variance, allowing agents to learn from "all-fail" rollout groups where standard advantage-based methods stall.
• Establishes four critical design rules to maintain agent motivation and avoid the **inaction trap**, ensuring safety constraints do not prevent task completion.
key_results: Achieves near-zero constraint violations while maintaining high task success rates.
Outperforms outcome-only training (RLVR) which violated constraints on nearly every episode.
Demonstrates superior sample efficiency by extracting signal from rollouts with uniform failure outcomes.
Shows that path-based penalties are more reliably verifiable and effective than progress-based potential rewards.
Successfully mitigates the inaction trap, where agents remain idle to avoid all possible penalties.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce RLVP to enable safer, more efficient agent training by combining dense path-based penalties with sparse outcome rewards.

title: Beware of Agentic Botnets: Scalable Untargeted Promptware Attacks via Universal and Transferable Adversarial HalluSquatting
summary: The growing adoption of agentic LLM applications has introduced a new threat previously named as promptware. While prior work has established that adversaries can exploit direct channels to LLM applications to apply promptware under weak threat models, many applications do not provide any direct channels that could be exploited for prompt injection beyond the Internet. This raises a question: can attackers exploit LLM applications at scale without any direct channels in practical threat models? In this work, we show that the inherent tendency of LLMs to hallucinate resource identifiers can be exploited to amplify untargeted promptware attacks that pull adversarial prompts at scale and could be exploited to establish a botnet. We introduce adversarial hallucination squatting, a technique in which attackers identify trending resources (e.g., popular repositories, popular skills, etc.), compute the LLM distribution of hallucinations on the trending resource names, and preemptively register them to host adversarial prompts. By leveraging the predictability and transferability of hallucinations across foundational LLMs and to application layers, adversaries can significantly amplify the reach of untargeted promptware under weak threat models and establish a botnet by exploiting LLM applications to install a bot on the device that pulled the compromised hallucinated resource from the Inter. We empirically demonstrate that hallucinated resource generation occurs at high rates, up to 85% in repository cloning scenarios and up to 100% in skill installation, and that these hallucinations transfer between foundational models and different prompts. We demonstrate the practicality of adversarial hallucination squatting against various production LLM applications with integrated terminals in their set of tools, achieving remote tool execution and remote code execution.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Adversarial HalluSquatting**, a novel attack vector that exploits the inherent tendency of LLMs to hallucinate resource identifiers (e.g., repositories, skills, or packages) to distribute malicious prompts.
- Demonstrates how attackers can build **agentic botnets** by preemptively registering these hallucinated names on public registries, enabling large-scale, untargeted attacks without direct input channel access.
- Analyzes the **transferability** of these hallucinations across different foundational models, showing that a single squatting attempt can compromise multiple application layers and ecosystems.
key_results: Identified hallucination rates up to 85% in repository cloning scenarios.
Achieved 100% hallucination rates in specific skill installation prompts.
Proved that hallucinated resource names are predictable and transfer between foundational LLMs.
Demonstrated practical Remote Code Execution (RCE) on production applications with integrated terminals.
Validated the feasibility of establishing botnets by exploiting LLM applications to install malicious 'bots' via hallucinated resources.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that LLM hallucinations can be weaponized via HalluSquatting to build scalable, untargeted agentic botnets.

title: Immersive Social Interaction with VR and LLM-Assisted Humanoids
summary: Humanoid robots can extend human presence to remote, constrained, or hazardous environments, but existing teleoperation interfaces often require physically demanding motion tracking or cognitively demanding low-level control. This paper presents an immersive teleoperation framework that integrates voice-controlled locomotion, VR-based manipulation, and bidirectional social interaction for whole-body humanoid control. Using Apple Vision Pro, the operator receives egocentric visual feedback, issues natural-language locomotion commands, and teleoperates the robot's arms and dexterous hands through wrist and finger tracking. An LLM-assisted voice-control module converts spoken instructions into high-level locomotion commands, while the manipulation module retargets human hand motions to the robot through inverse kinematics and PD control. The system also records multimodal data, including egocentric RGB observations, voice/text commands, joint states, hand motions, and eye-gaze signals, supporting future imitation learning and autonomy. We evaluate the framework on a Unitree H1 humanoid equipped with dexterous hands in manipulation and social interaction tasks. Results show that novice users can successfully operate the system after brief familiarization, achieving 80\% success in object manipulation and 70\% success in a social cube-passing task. These results demonstrate the potential of immersive, language-assisted teleoperation as an accessible interface for humanoid interaction, remote assistance, and multimodal data collection.

processed.summary:
* Integrates **Apple Vision Pro** for immersive VR teleoperation, providing egocentric visual feedback and finger-level tracking for dexterous **humanoid control**.
* Utilizes an **LLM-assisted voice-control module** to parse natural language instructions into high-level locomotion commands, significantly reducing operator cognitive load.
* Establishes a **multimodal data collection pipeline** including RGB, eye-gaze, and joint states to facilitate future **imitation learning** and autonomous behavior models.
key_results: Novice operators achieved a 80% success rate in manual object manipulation tasks.
The system reached a 70% success rate in social cube-passing interaction tests.
Successfully mapped human wrist and finger motions to robotic dexterous hands using IK and PD control.
Voice-to-command LLM integration enabled natural-language locomotion without physical controllers.
Demonstrated successful teleoperation of the Unitree H1 humanoid in remote social scenarios.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop an LLM-assisted VR framework enabling novice users to perform complex social and manipulation tasks with humanoids.

title: InductWave: Inductive Multi-Hop Logical Query Answering on Knowledge Graphs
summary: Logical Multi-Hop Query Answering over Knowledge Graphs (KGs) can be formulated as querying, with an implicit completeness assumption. Current works mainly focus on Existential First Order Logic (EFO) queries. These EFO queries contain conjunction, disjunction, and negation operators. Most existing works employ transductive reasoning, meaning they are not capable of reasoning over entities unseen during training. In the real world, there is a resource scarcity, and we cannot train a model with all the nodes of a large KG. Hence, we propose InductWave, a wavelet-based inductive embedding method for logical query answering on large KGs. Here, the training graph consists of fewer nodes than the test graph. Our model performs on par with the baseline models while having half the number of message-passing layers. It outperforms all of them in most cases, with 75% of the layers. These fewer resource requirements enable us to evaluate InductWave on massive graphs, such as Wiki-KG. We test our model using extensive experiments across varying train-test graph proportions of the FB15k-(237) dataset, comparing it with the state-of-the-art models. The code and datasets for the model are available at https://github.com/kracr/inductwave/.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **InductWave**, a novel **wavelet-based inductive embedding** framework designed for Multi-Hop Logical Query Answering (LQA) on large Knowledge Graphs.
* Solves the limitation of **transductive reasoning** by allowing the model to perform inference on entities and nodes that were entirely absent during the training phase.
* Utilizes an efficient architecture that maintains high accuracy while requiring only **50% to 75% of the message-passing layers** compared to established baselines.
* Enables scalable reasoning on **massive datasets** like Wiki-KG, which are typically too large for traditional resource-intensive GNN models.
key_results: Achieves competitive performance with significantly reduced computational overhead.
Successfully handles inductive reasoning for EFO logic queries including conjunction, disjunction, and negation.
Outperforms state-of-the-art models on the FB15k-237 dataset across various train-test proportions.
Demonstrates superior scalability on the Wiki-KG dataset compared to transductive methods.
Maintains high accuracy even with a substantial reduction in message-passing depth.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: KRACR researchers introduce InductWave, a wavelet-based method enabling efficient, inductive logical reasoning across massive, unseen knowledge graphs.

title: Hyperuniform systems are maximally irreversible
summary: Hyperuniform systems, defined by the anomalous suppression of large-scale density fluctuations, are a paradigm of non-equilibrium self-assembly. While mechanisms underlying the self-assembly of hyperuniform states have been widely studied, the energetics of this process remain unexplored. This raises a fundamental question: what is the energetic cost of self-assembling a hyperuniform system? Here, we address this question across several noisy particle systems drawn from soft matter and machine learning, in which hyperuniformity can be induced by tuning noise correlations. Despite their distinct microscopic dynamics, we uncover a universal behavior across all systems: hyperuniform states are maximally irreversible, as quantified by the entropy production rate. Further, we develop a path integral formulation of the entropy production rate directly from the microscopic dynamics, which explains our observations. Our work establishes a direct link between emergent long-range structure and time irreversibility and opens a new avenue of probing the energetic cost of hyperuniform self-assembly, ubiquitous across physics, biology, and materials science.

processed.summary:
- Explores the **energetic cost** and **entropy production rate** of self-assembling **hyperuniform systems** within soft matter and machine learning particle models.
- Proposes a **path integral formulation** derived from microscopic dynamics to quantify the link between long-range structure and time irreversibility.
- Demonstrates that hyperuniform states—characterized by suppressed large-scale density fluctuations—are **maximally irreversible** across various noisy systems.
- Establishes a universal behavior where tuning **noise correlations** to induce structural order results in peak energy dissipation.
key_results: Hyperuniform states produce the maximum entropy rate compared to any other structural configuration in the studied systems.
The relationship between structural order and irreversibility holds universal across both physical soft matter and ML-based particle dynamics.
A new path integral framework allows for the direct calculation of entropy production from microscopic noisy dynamics.
Hyperuniformity can be systematically induced by specific tuning of noise correlations within the system.
The study provides a fundamental thermodynamic limit on the cost of creating long-range suppressed density fluctuations.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that hyperuniform self-assembly in machine learning and physical systems correlates with maximal entropy production rates.

title: DeLS-Spec: Decoupled Long-Short Contexts for Parallel Speculative Drafting
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by drafting multiple tokens and verifying them in parallel. Block-parallel drafters such as DFlash further improve drafting efficiency by predicting an entire block in one pass, but their position-wise predictions lack explicit intra-block causal conditioning. Recent methods such as Domino and DSpark attempt to introduce such causality into block-parallel drafting, but they require training the draft model from scratch, which limits their flexibility and increases training cost. We propose DeLS-Spec, a decoupled long-short context speculative decoding method. DeLS-Spec treats the fixed DFlash model as a long-context expert and introduces a lightweight local head as a short-context expert. The local head can be trained independently with a standard next-token prediction objective, without joint training with the target model or the DFlash backbone, leading to extremely low training cost. At inference time, DeLS-Spec combines long-context and short-context logits, and the local head is not tied to a specific DFlash checkpoint, making the method more modular and flexible. Experiments on Qwen3 models show that DeLS-Spec consistently improves speedup and average acceptance length over DFlash across math, code, and dialogue benchmarks.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **DeLS-Spec**, a novel speculative decoding framework that decouples **long-context and short-context** processing to improve block-parallel drafting efficiency.
- Features a **lightweight local head** acting as a short-context expert to provide intra-block causal conditioning without retraining the fixed **DFlash backbone**.
- Implements an **independent training objective** using standard next-token prediction, allowing the local head to be swapped across different model checkpoints for high **modularity**.
- Optimizes the inference pipeline by combining **logits** from both experts, addressing the position-wise prediction limitations of existing block-parallel drafters.
key_results: Consistent inference speedup across math, code, and dialogue benchmarks using Qwen3 models.
Increased average acceptance length compared to the baseline DFlash method.
Significantly lower training costs by avoiding joint training with the target model.
Improved intra-block causality through the integration of the short-context local head.
Validation of decoupled architecture flexibility as the local head is not tied to specific model checkpoints.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Researchers introduce DeLS-Spec to accelerate LLM inference by decoupling context experts for more efficient parallel speculative drafting.

title: Transformer-based segmentation of prosodic boundaries in Brazilian Portuguese
summary: Automatic prosodic segmentation identifies boundaries between speech units from acoustic and linguistic evidence. Although recent deep learning approaches have produced strong results for English, automatic segmentation for Brazilian Portuguese (BP) still relies mostly on rule-based or traditional machine-learning methods. This paper presents SAMPA, a Whisper-based segmenter that transcribes BP speech while inserting explicit markers for terminal prosodic boundaries. We fine-tune Whisper large-v3 on manually segmented recordings from the NURC-SP dataset and evaluate different training and test-time filtering configurations, including out-of-distribution testing on the MuPe-Diversidades dataset. SAMPA achieves competitive boundary-detection performance across settings, with the best models reaching F1=0.731 on the held-out test split and F1=0.796 on MuPe-Diversidades. Finally, through n-gram and acoustic-visual analyses, we show that our model follows morphosyntactic, semantic, and prosodic cues for detecting prosodic boundaries.

processed.summary:
- Researchers introduce **SAMPA**, a specialized segmenter built by fine-tuning the **Whisper large-v3** transformer model for **prosodic boundary detection** in Brazilian Portuguese.
- The architecture utilizes an **encoder-decoder** framework to simultaneously transcribe speech and insert explicit markers for terminal boundaries, moving beyond traditional rule-based methods.
- The model was trained on the **NURC-SP dataset** and incorporates **multi-modal cues**, including acoustic, morphosyntactic, and semantic features to determine speech units.
- Evaluation includes **out-of-distribution (OOD)** testing to ensure generalizability across different speech contexts and recording conditions.
key_results: Achieved a peak F1-score of 0.731 on the NURC-SP held-out test set.
Reached an improved F1-score of 0.796 on the MuPe-Diversidades OOD dataset.
Successfully adapted Whisper large-v3 for specialized linguistic segmentation tasks via fine-tuning.
Confirmed through n-gram analysis that the model captures complex morphosyntactic and semantic patterns.
Established a new state-of-the-art benchmark for automatic prosodic segmentation in Brazilian Portuguese.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Brazilian researchers fine-tune Whisper large-v3 to achieve state-of-the-art prosodic boundary segmentation for Portuguese speech units.

title: Reason Less, Verify More: Deterministic Gates Recover a Silent Policy-Violation Failure Mode in Tool-Using LLM Agents
summary:
Tool-using LLM agents can violate the very policies they are deployed to enforce while appearing to complete the task successfully. In policy-permissive environments, a tool may execute any well-formed call even when the corresponding state transition is forbidden by domain policy. The result is a silent wrong state (a booking cancelled, a passenger count changed, a claim acted on without verification) that neither the tool nor the agent's self-report exposes.
  We study this failure mode in the $τ^2$-bench airline domain. On a budget agent, 78% of observed failures are silent wrong-state failures with no tool error, and the aggregate failure rate is reproducible across disjoint seeds, not sampling noise. We then evaluate a lightweight intervention: deterministic, read-only pre-execution gates that inspect the proposed call and current state before allowing a write. A four-gate suite raises full-benchmark success from 29.6% to 42.0% on gpt-4o-mini (+12.4pp; paired task-level bootstrap P=0.0012), and the lift reproduces on a disjoint 15-seed set (+12.3pp; P=0.0008).
  The effect is concentrated where the gates fire: on the 26/50 firing tasks, success rises by +19.2pp, while movement on the 24 non-firing tasks does not exclude zero. Two negative controls (a self-enforcing retail domain and BFCL) bound the mechanism: gates help when tools are policy-permissive and add little where tools already self-enforce. As suggestive evidence, not a central claim, the same failure mode persists at the frontier: gpt-5.2 at default reasoning still attempts policy-violating writes, and the same suite improves success from 61.2% to 71.6% (+10.4pp; P=0.020; n=5, no replication). The contribution is a bounded evaluation and reliability result: deterministic gates do not guarantee task success, but they can deterministically prevent a known class of silent policy-violating writes at the action boundary.

processed.summary:
Identifies a critical failure mode of **silent policy-violations** where tool-using agents execute syntactically correct but domain-forbidden actions that result in incorrect states without generating errors.
 Proposes the use of **deterministic, read-only pre-execution gates** that validate proposed tool calls and current system states before allowing write operations to proceed.
 Demonstrates that architectural constraints at the **action boundary** are more effective at enforcing complex domain policies than relying solely on the LLM's internal reasoning or self-reporting.
 Validates the approach across multiple models, showing significant success rate recovery in **policy-permissive environments** where tools lack native enforcement mechanisms.
key_results: Silent failures caused 78% of observed errors in budget agents on the tau-bench airline domain.
Deterministic gates improved gpt-4o-mini success rates from 29.6% to 42.0% (+12.4pp).
On tasks where gates specifically fired, success rates increased by 19.2 percentage points.
Frontier model testing showed success increasing from 61.2% to 71.6% using the same gate suite.
Gates provide high utility for policy-permissive tools but offer marginal gains for tools with built-in enforcement.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Tau-bench researchers demonstrate that deterministic pre-execution gates prevent silent policy violations and improve tool-using agent reliability across models.

title: Multi-Agent Robotic Control with Onboard Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision Language Models (VLMs) and Vision Language Action (VLA) models have shown promise in robotic control. Yet, they face significant challenges regarding explainability, generalization, and compute requirements. This paper presents a Multi-Agent System (MAS) architecture that addresses these limitations by deploying specialized agents on onboard hardware - eliminating dependence on external compute. The system controls a multi-purpose autonomous mobile manipulator in a simulated industrial warehouse, fulfilling five task categories: safety inspection, warehouse maintenance, warehouse search, package quality verification, and responding to human requests. Compact VLMs (3-20B parameters) are used throughout, with fine-tuning applied to improve package inspection accuracy. A novel "Megamind" orchestration agent mitigates context retention issues inherent to long-horizon planning with smaller models. The system was validated in a hardware-in-the-loop simulation using an AMD Ryzen(TM) AI mini PC. Results demonstrate that a fully onboard MAS architecture is a viable, cost-efficient alternative to cloud-dependent deployments, with strong potential for real-world transfer. The simulation environment has been released as open source under the Apache 2.0 licence.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a **Multi-Agent System (MAS)** architecture for autonomous robotics, utilizing **onboard compact VLMs** (3-20B parameters) to eliminate cloud latency and privacy risks.
• Introduces the **Megamind** orchestration agent to handle long-horizon planning and context retention, effectively managing specialized sub-agents for complex workflows.
• Demonstrates the efficacy of **fine-tuned Small Language Models (SLMs)** for high-precision tasks like package quality verification while maintaining low compute overhead on edge hardware.
• Validated in a **hardware-in-the-loop simulation** using an AMD Ryzen AI mini PC, proving that local deployment is a cost-effective alternative to cloud-dependent systems.
key_results: Successful deployment of 3-20B parameter VLMs on localized mini PC hardware without external compute.
Improved package inspection accuracy through targeted model fine-tuning on specialized visual datasets.
Effective long-horizon task execution via the 'Megamind' orchestration layer mitigating context loss.
Achievement of five distinct industrial task categories including safety inspection and human response.
Open-source release of the simulation environment under Apache 2.0 to facilitate community research.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop a multi-agent onboard VLM system enabling autonomous, cloud-independent robotic control for complex industrial warehouse tasks.

title: Coexistence and manipulation of multiple singularities in a reconfigurable non-Hermitian metasurface
summary: Non-Hermitian frameworks extend conventional Hermitian physics, offering a powerful paradigm for describing open systems. Central to this field are various singularities within the complex parameter space, such as exceptional points (EPs) and scattering zeros, which dictate exotic physical behaviors. As research shifts from isolated singularities toward multi-singularity interactions, conventional planar metasurfaces remain constrained by limited tuning dimensions. Here, we propose a mirror-coupled design that maps a metasurface into a quasi-high-dimensional parameter space. By employing a metallic plane to generate image resonators, this scheme multiplies the system degrees of freedom without increasing the number of physical resonators. Its implementation on a reconfigurable platform integrated with PIN diodes yields the coexistence and manipulation of an EP and multiple reflection zeros. Through simulations and microwave experiments, we characterize the dynamic evolution of these singularities and exploit their synergistic effects for two distinct applications. First, for tunable absorption, multiple reflection zeros are spectrally coordinated to achieve a near-perfect absorption band exceeding $99.9\%$ across the X-band, thereby dynamically suppressing target scattering. Second, for enhanced sensing, a reflection zero couples with the EP to form a hybrid singularity. This hybrid state inherits the power-law sensitivity of the EP while substantially boosting robustness against fluctuations, resolving the conventional trade-off between sensitivity and stability and simplifying detection to direct peak tracking rather than complex multimode eigenvalue fitting. Our work provides a general methodology to circumvent parameter competition among non-Hermitian singularities, opening new avenues for multifunctional metadevices across the electromagnetic spectrum.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **mirror-coupled metasurface** architecture that utilizes a metallic plane to generate image resonators, effectively multiplying system degrees of freedom.
- Implements a reconfigurable platform using **PIN diodes** to enable the simultaneous coexistence and dynamic manipulation of **Exceptional Points (EPs)** and reflection zeros.
- Introduces a **hybrid singularity** state that combines power-law sensitivity with high robustness, allowing for direct peak tracking instead of complex eigenvalue fitting.
- Achieves a near-perfect **dynamic absorption band** exceeding 99.9 percent across the X-band, demonstrating superior control over target scattering.
key_results: Mapping of metasurfaces into quasi-high-dimensional parameter spaces via mirror-coupling.
Successful coexistence of an Exceptional Point and multiple reflection zeros on a single platform.
Demonstration of tunable microwave absorption exceeding 99.9 percent efficiency.
Resolution of the sensitivity-stability trade-off in non-Hermitian sensing applications.
Simplification of sensing methodology through direct spectral peak tracking.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a mirror-coupled metasurface design that manipulates non-Hermitian singularities to enhance electromagnetic sensing and absorption performance.

title: Heterogeneity-Adaptive Diffusion Schrodinger Bridge for PET-Guided Whole-Body MRI Translation
summary: While whole-body multimodal medical imaging scanners have been increasingly recognized for more effective medical applications, the excessive long acquisition time in PET-MR scanning is a major obstacle in more efficient clinical practice. Deep learning-based MRI translation provides a potential solution to reduce scan duration. However, current models often focus on specific anatomical regions and face challenges for whole-body scans that consists of highly heterogeneous feature distributions mainly due to (1) different anatomical regions across whole-body, and (2) lesions or pathological tissues. This paper tackles the challenges through a novel Heterogeneity-Adaptive Diffusion Schrodinger Bridge (HA-DSB) framework. By explicitly modeling translation as stochastic transport between source and target distributions, HA-DSB incorporates region context embeddings derived from a vision-language model (VLM) to enable region-specific modeling. To enhance fidelity of the pathological tissue, lesion-aware metabolic prior from PET is integrated directly into the bridge dynamics through a dual-stage guidance mechanism. Specifically, a PET-guided noise modulation module adaptively scales spatial diffusion perturbations during the forward process, while PET features are leveraged during the reverse process to selectively amplify lesion-relevant structures via an attention mechanism. Experiments demonstrate the superiority of our method across different body regions in whole-body MRI translation and show improved translation quality in lesion areas under PET guidance. Our code is available at Github.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **HA-DSB**, a novel framework that models whole-body MRI translation as a stochastic transport problem using **Diffusion Schrodinger Bridges**.
• Incorporates **Vision-Language Model (VLM)** context embeddings to enable region-specific modeling, addressing the heterogeneity of anatomical structures across the body.
• Implements a **dual-stage PET guidance** mechanism, featuring noise modulation in the forward process and attention-based amplification in the reverse process.
• Enhances the fidelity of **pathological tissues** and lesions by integrating metabolic priors directly into the bridge dynamics.
key_results: Achieved superior translation quality across diverse anatomical regions compared to regional models.
Improved preservation of lesion-specific structures using PET metabolic priors.
Demonstrated the effectiveness of VLM embeddings in handling spatial feature heterogeneity.
Validated a dual-stage guidance mechanism for scaling spatial diffusion perturbations.
Provided a potential solution to reduce PET-MR scan duration through efficient AI translation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop HA-DSB, using VLMs and PET guidance to enable accurate, heterogeneity-adaptive whole-body MRI translation.

title: Agentic Data Environments
summary:
Autonomous agents promise substantial gains in speed, scale, and labor efficiency, but their failures can impose abrupt and often irreversible costs. The central challenge for agentic automation is therefore to increase the benefits of automation while bounding the consequences of failure.
  While databases remain central to modern computing, agents operate over a broader data environment spanning files, APIs, applications, and system state. In this talk, I will outline early work on Agentic Data Environments -- the execution substrate in which agents operate -- that both amplify agent capabilities and enforce safety guarantees. This perspective reframes data systems from passive stores of state into active substrates for safe, reliable execution.

processed.summary:
* Proposes **Agentic Data Environments** as the primary execution substrate for autonomous agents, integrating files, APIs, and system states beyond traditional databases.
* Aims to mitigate the **irreversible costs** of agent failure by embedding safety guarantees and operational boundaries directly into the data layer.
* Reframes data systems from **passive stores** into active, reliable substrates designed specifically for agentic reasoning and execution loops.
key_results: Identification of system state and APIs as critical components of the agent's data environment.
Categorization of agent failure costs as a primary bottleneck for automation adoption.
Proposal of environment-level safety constraints to bound agent behavior.
Transformation of data systems from passive storage to active execution participants.
Framework for amplifying agent speed and scale through specialized substrate architecture.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose Agentic Data Environments to transform passive storage into active, safe execution substrates for autonomous agents.

title: When Prompts Ignore Structure: Graph-Based Attribute Reasoning for Calibrated VLMs
summary: Reliable confidence estimation remains a key limitation of test-time adaptation in vision-language models (VLMs), where prompt tuning improves zero-shot accuracy but often degrades calibration due to entropy-driven overconfidence. Prior approaches mitigate this using LLM-derived class attributes and contrastive regularization, yet treat attributes independently, ignoring their relational structure. We propose ARGTCA, which represents (class, attribute) pairs as nodes in a Symbolic Attribute Graph and trains a Graph Attention Network (GAT) using contrastive objectives to produce structurally informed embeddings that capture inter-attribute dependencies. We introduce two attribute selection strategies: ARGTCA-DIV for intra-class diversity and ARGTCA-DISC for inter-class discrimination. Experiments across nine benchmarks show that ARGTCA-DIV reduces average Expected Calibration Error (ECE) by approximately ~37% over baselines, while ARGTCA-DISC consistently performs as the second-best variant, reducing average ECE by approximately ~17% over baselines. These results suggest that modeling symbolic attribute interactions provides a principled approach for reliable test-time adaptation in VLMs.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **ARGTCA**, a framework for test-time adaptation in **Vision-Language Models (VLMs)** that utilizes a **Symbolic Attribute Graph** to capture inter-attribute dependencies.
- Integrates a **Graph Attention Network (GAT)** trained via contrastive objectives to produce structurally informed embeddings, addressing the limitations of treating attributes as independent entities.
- Introduces two distinct selection strategies, **ARGTCA-DIV** and **ARGTCA-DISC**, which optimize for intra-class diversity and inter-class discrimination respectively.
- Demonstrates that modeling symbolic interactions significantly improves **model calibration (ECE)**, mitigating the overconfidence issues inherent in entropy-driven prompt tuning.
key_results: ARGTCA-DIV reduced average Expected Calibration Error (ECE) by approximately 37% across nine benchmarks.
ARGTCA-DISC consistently outperformed standard baselines, reducing ECE by approximately 17%.
Successfully mapped (class, attribute) pairs as nodes in a graph to capture complex relational structures.
Validated that structural attribute reasoning is more effective for calibration than independent attribute prompts.
Established a new state-of-the-art for reliable test-time adaptation in zero-shot VLM tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce ARGTCA to improve VLM calibration by modeling class attributes as structured symbolic graphs during prompt tuning.

title: MIRA-Math: A Benchmark for Minimal Information Requesting and Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Mathematical reasoning benchmarks typically provide all facts needed to solve each problem, while interactive benchmarks often mix reasoning with tools, retrieval, and long-horizon dialogue. We introduce MIRA-Math, a benchmark for a narrower diagnostic capability: solving mathematical problems whose full latent state has a unique answer, but whose solver-facing view is missing exactly one necessary atomic fact. The solver must request the missing information in natural language under a strict budget and then integrate the returned fact into an exact final answer. A fixed constrained LLM responder sees only the dataset-provided atomic fact and must either offer the quoted fact when the request matches it, or decline otherwise. Thus, instance generation, typed hint specifications, validation, and final-answer verification are deterministic, while request metrics are measured under a fixed LLM-mediated responder channel. MIRA-Math contains 2{,}310 generated instances from 22 typed mathematical families spanning algebra, probability, linear systems, discrete structures, signal processing, Markov chains, circuits, interpolation, and numerical boundary-value problems. Experiments across frontier and small models show that request success and final-answer accuracy are separable: models may ask for the right fact yet fail the downstream computation, or fail before obtaining the canonical hint. We release generators, verifiers, prompts, run metadata, and dataset documentation to support reproducible evaluation of minimal information requesting in mathematical reasoning.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **MIRA-Math**, a diagnostic benchmark focused on the ability of LLMs to identify and request a **single missing atomic fact** required to solve a math problem.
- Features a **deterministic responder framework** where a constrained LLM provides the hidden fact only if the solver's natural language request precisely matches the missing information.
- Spans **22 mathematical families** including linear systems, Markov chains, and signal processing, ensuring a broad test of diverse reasoning capabilities.
- Decouples the **information-seeking phase** from the **computational execution**, allowing researchers to pinpoint whether models fail at retrieval or reasoning.
key_results: Released a dataset of 2,310 generated instances across diverse fields like probability and numerical boundary-value problems.
Identified that request success and final-answer accuracy are separable metrics in frontier models.
Observed that models frequently ask for the correct missing fact but fail the subsequent downstream calculation.
Established a fixed, reproducible LLM-mediated channel for measuring interactive request quality.
Provided a full suite of open-source generators and verifiers to support standardized AI evaluation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce MIRA-Math to benchmark how effectively LLMs request missing information to solve complex mathematical reasoning tasks.

title: TF-Engram: A Train-Free Engram with SSD-Backed Memory for Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) store factual knowledge and domain-specific patterns implicitly in dense Transformer parameters, making knowledge expansion costly through pretraining, fine-tuning, retrieval augmentation, or longer contexts. Engram-style memory offers a compact hidden-state injection pathway, but existing GPU-resident designs often rely on hash-based compression, causing unrelated phrases to collide in shared slots and weakening phrase-level semantic fidelity. We present TF-Engram, a train-free Engram system that constructs phrase-specific semantic memory offline from external corpora, stores large memory tables across a GPU--DRAM--SSD hierarchy, and uses Early-Exit Guided Predictive Prefetching to hide external-memory latency during autoregressive decoding. On Qwen3-0.6B, TF-Engram improves the average downstream score from 57.6 to 59.4, outperforming both the frozen backbone and a parameter-matched LoRA baseline. System evaluation shows that large TF-Engram tables can be built with moderate offline cost, SSD-backed storage substantially reduces GPU memory demand, and predictive prefetching recovers much of the throughput loss caused by external memory access. These results demonstrate that static phrase memory can be integrated into LLM inference as a scalable, train-free, and low-overhead system component.

processed.summary:
• **TF-Engram** introduces a train-free semantic memory system that constructs phrase-level mappings from external corpora to inject factual knowledge without retraining.
• The architecture implements a **GPU-DRAM-SSD memory hierarchy**, enabling massive knowledge bases to be stored on cost-effective storage while minimizing high-cost VRAM usage.
• It utilizes **Early-Exit Guided Predictive Prefetching** to overlap SSD data retrieval with early-layer transformer computation, effectively hiding I/O latency during decoding.
• Experimental results on **Qwen3-0.6B** show the system outperforms both frozen backbones and LoRA-tuned baselines in downstream tasks through better semantic fidelity.
key_results: Improved Qwen3-0.6B downstream score from 57.6 to 59.4, surpassing parameter-matched LoRA.
Eliminated phrase collisions inherent in hash-based systems by using a dedicated SSD-backed hierarchy.
Demonstrated that predictive prefetching recovers significant throughput lost to external memory access.
Reduced GPU memory demand substantially by offloading large memory tables to SSD and DRAM.
Established a scalable, train-free framework for augmenting LLMs with static phrase-specific memory.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce TF-Engram, a train-free SSD-backed memory architecture that boosts LLM knowledge retrieval while maintaining high inference throughput.

title: A Large Language Model-Driven Agent-Based Modeling Framework with Multi-Round Communication for Simulating Vaccine Opinion Dynamics
summary: Recently, Large Language Models (LLMs) have been utilized in various applications of computational social science and provide the possibility to integrate such models into agent-based modeling to explore the cognitive processes. However, how specific cognitive modules drive individual decisions and macro-level opinion dynamics remains unclear. Therefore, this study introduces a framework that integrates an LLM (Qwen3-8B) into agent-based modeling to investigate this problem, using vaccination opinion dynamics as a case study. We utilize this framework to simulate opinion dynamics among agents with heterogeneous profiles and social networks, evaluating scenarios by enabling different cognitive modules: a memory module and a prompt diversity module. The simulation results reveal that different cognitive modules have opposite impacts on our emergent opinion. Furthermore, the framework reproduces the non-linear behavior patterns of social influence observed in existing research, demonstrating our framework's validity and potential to reach the level 3 validation of agent-based models.

processed.summary:
- Introduces an **Agent-Based Modeling (ABM)** framework that integrates **Local LLMs** (Qwen3-8B) to simulate micro-level cognitive processes and macro-level social dynamics.
- Features a **multi-round communication** protocol between agents with heterogeneous profiles, allowing for realistic social network interactions and opinion exchange.
- Implements specialized architectural components including a **Memory Module** and a **Prompt Diversity Module** to analyze their distinct impacts on emergent group behaviors.
- Achieves **Level 3 validation** for agent-based models by successfully reproducing observed non-linear patterns of social influence found in real-world sociological data.
key_results: Demonstrated that different cognitive modules (Memory vs. Prompt Diversity) have diametrically opposite effects on emergent group opinions.
Reproduced complex, non-linear social influence behaviors previously observed in empirical social science research.
Validated the use of 8B-parameter local models for high-fidelity social simulation and decision-making tasks.
Established a framework for mapping individual LLM-driven cognitive processes to macro-level sociological phenomena.
Confirmed the framework's robustness across heterogeneous agent profiles and varied social network topologies.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce an LLM-driven agent framework using Qwen-8B to simulate non-linear vaccine opinion dynamics and social influence.

title: Sparse Delta Memory: Scaling the State of Linear RNNs through Sparsity
summary: Linear attention models allow a fixed state size and a fixed amount of compute per token. However, due to their limited state size, linear attention models fall behind in long-context recall compared to softmax-attention-based transformer architectures. Increasing the state size of linear attention improves recall performance but at the cost of higher FLOPs. In this work, we introduce Sparse Delta Memory (SDM), an architecture that scales the hidden state of gated linear RNNs to orders of magnitude higher capacity using a sparse addressing scheme. SDM extends the Gated DeltaNet architecture by replacing the dense key-value outer product with sparse reads and writes to a large explicit memory. We show that, under an isoFLOP constraint and with an identical number of parameters, a higher state memory capacity significantly improves performance on in-context learning and long-context retrieval tasks. Moreover, by learning the initial state of the SDM memory and therefore using it as a parametric memory, we show that the model further improves on a wide range of common-knowledge and reasoning tasks.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Sparse Delta Memory (SDM)**, an architecture that scales the hidden state of **Gated Linear RNNs** using a sparse addressing scheme to increase capacity by orders of magnitude.
- Replaces traditional dense key-value updates with **sparse reads and writes**, decoupling state size from computational cost (FLOPs) and parameter count.
- Enables the hidden state to function as **parametric memory** by learning initial states, which significantly enhances the model's performance on common-knowledge and reasoning tasks.
- Bridges the performance gap between **Linear Attention** models and standard Softmax Transformers for intensive long-context retrieval scenarios.
key_results: Scales hidden state capacity by orders of magnitude under isoFLOP constraints.
Significantly improves performance on in-context learning (ICL) compared to dense linear models.
Enhances long-context retrieval capabilities while maintaining fixed compute per token.
Demonstrates superior performance on common-knowledge benchmarks via learned initial memory states.
Maintains identical parameter counts while providing the benefits of a much larger stateful memory.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Tsinghua University researchers introduce Sparse Delta Memory to scale Linear RNN states, significantly enhancing long-context retrieval and reasoning efficiency.

title: MMAgent-R$^2$: Learning to Rerank and Reject for Agentic mRAG
summary: Knowledge-based Visual Question Answering (KB-VQA) requires models to retrieve visual entities matching the query image from large-scale encyclopedic knowledge bases and answer related questions. Existing multimodal Retrieval Augmented Generation (mRAG) methods rely on global visual features to match candidate entities, yet when the knowledge base contains numerous visually similar entities, the retriever struggles to distinguish them, populating the candidate set with visually similar but factually mismatched distractors. Since subsequent processing steps such as noise filtering are also confined to this fixed candidate set, errors from failed retrieval inevitably propagate to the final answer. To address these challenges, we propose MMAgent-R$^2$, an agentic mRAG framework that integrates visual reranking and active rejection as its internal verification mechanism. Visual reranking directly compares query and candidate images, capturing discriminative details beyond textual descriptions to precisely identify the target entity among similar candidates; active rejection discards unreliable results and retrieves additional candidates when no confident match is found, moving beyond the fixed candidate pool. We design a composite reward function with step-level verification rewards and achieve joint optimization of external retrieval, internal verification, and answer generation via GRPO training. Experiments on InfoSeek, E-VQA, and MMhops demonstrate that \ours{} achieves state-of-the-art performance, with particularly notable advantages in challenging retrieval scenarios and complex multi-image multi-hop reasoning tasks.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **MMAgent-R2**, an agentic multimodal RAG (mRAG) framework designed to handle fine-grained visual discrimination in large-scale knowledge bases.
- Implements an internal verification mechanism featuring **Visual Reranking** for direct image-to-image comparison and **Active Rejection** to trigger iterative retrieval when confidence is low.

key_results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on InfoSeek, E-VQA, and MMhops benchmarks.
Visual reranking effectively filters visually similar but factually mismatched distractors that confound standard retrievers.
Active rejection mechanism successfully moves beyond fixed candidate pools by requesting additional retrieval steps.
Joint optimization of agentic actions and generation was achieved through step-level verification rewards.
Significant accuracy gains recorded in challenging multi-image and multi-hop visual question answering tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce MMAgent-R2, an agentic mRAG framework using visual reranking and active rejection for SOTA visual reasoning.

title: Interpretable Uncertainty for Adaptive Retrieval and Reasoning in Question Answering
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve a strong performance in question answering (QA), but remain prone to hallucinations and suffer from limited transparency. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) can improve factuality, yet decisions about when and how to retrieve from external resources are typically based on opaque policies or computationally inefficient multi-step prompting procedures. We propose an uncertainty-aware framework for adaptive QA based on explicit signals derived from LLM internal representations. We distinguish between knowledge insufficiency and knowledge ambiguity or conflict, and efficiently estimate these from hidden states in a single forward pass. These signals guide system behaviour: RAG is triggered when knowledge is insufficient, while additional reasoning is applied when ambiguity or conflict is high. By grounding adaptive decisions in decomposed and efficiently estimable uncertainty signals, this approach provides a transparent and practical alternative to existing retrieval and reasoning strategies supporting the design of interpretable user-facing tools.

processed.summary:
- Proposes an **uncertainty-aware framework** that uses internal LLM hidden states to guide adaptive retrieval and reasoning decisions.
- Distinguishes between **knowledge insufficiency** and **knowledge ambiguity**, allowing for granular control over system behavior.
- Implements a highly efficient **single forward pass** estimation of uncertainty signals, bypassing the need for expensive multi-step prompting.
- Enhances **RAG transparency** by providing explicit, interpretable triggers for when external data is required versus when internal reasoning is needed.
key_results: Successfully decomposed uncertainty into actionable signals for insufficiency and ambiguity.
Reduced computational overhead by replacing multi-step prompting with internal representation analysis.
Improved RAG efficiency by triggering retrieval only when internal knowledge was demonstrably lacking.
Demonstrated that hidden state signals can effectively resolve conflicting or ambiguous knowledge through targeted reasoning.
Provided a framework for more interpretable and reliable user-facing generative AI tools.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose using internal hidden states to adaptively trigger RAG or reasoning based on interpretable uncertainty signals.

title: Physics-Audited Agentic Discovery in Scientific Machine Learning
summary: In agentic scientific machine learning (SciML), large language model (LLM) agents can discover surrogate models and select one by an automated score, typically an error metric. A low error, however, does not establish that the predicted fields satisfy the physics that matter for mechanics, such as boundary conditions, superposition, stiffness scaling, or causality. We introduce Physics-Audited Agentic SciML (PA-SciML), a verification-first workflow for agentic SciML discovery. The workflow fixes a scoring evaluator before search, derives reviewable machine-checkable physics requirements, checks each trained candidate on its outputs, and separately searches prescribed input ranges or measured load-history spans for high-violation cases without reference solution fields. A surrogate is reported as verified only under the stated checks. When enabled, the workflow also adds advisory numerical probes before training and tests one modeling change at a time to record which isolated edits are associated with score gains before reuse. In the reported computational-solid-mechanics numerical examples, the static elasticity run selects a surrogate with lower validation error than the error-only baseline while both selected models pass the common linear-elastic checks. In the transient elastodynamics run, an error-only baseline with similar mean error fails a stricter causality check by responding to future parts of the loading history, while the selected surrogate passes the stated checks. The main distinction is per-candidate physics evidence on predicted fields, not a richer aggregate score.

processed.summary:
• PA-SciML introduces a **verification-first workflow** for agentic scientific machine learning (SciML), moving beyond simple error metrics to prioritize physical consistency.
• The architecture employs **LLM agents** to discover surrogate models while a fixed scoring evaluator audits outputs against machine-checkable requirements like **boundary conditions** and **causality**.
• The framework utilizes **advisory numerical probes** and isolated modeling edits to determine which specific architectural changes correlate with performance gains.
• It addresses the failure of error-only baselines in **transient elastodynamics** by detecting models that erroneously respond to future loading history.
key_results: Creation of a 'Physics-Audited' discovery loop for automated surrogate model selection.
Implementation of machine-checkable checks for superposition, stiffness scaling, and causality.
Demonstrated detection of causality violations in low-error models in elastodynamics.
Achieved lower validation errors in static elasticity compared to error-only baseline models.
Established a method for searching input ranges for high-violation cases without reference solution fields.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce PA-SciML to verify agentic scientific discovery using machine-checkable physics constraints for improved reliability.

title: Quantitative DFT+DMFT description of spectra and transport in the moderately correlated metal SrVO$_3$
summary: A quantitative, material-specific account of spectral and transport properties remains a central challenge in the theory of strongly correlated materials. Combining density functional theory with dynamical mean-field theory (DFT+DMFT) has proven to be a powerful approach for treating electron correlation effects and material specificity on an equal footing. Here, we examine the single-particle spectra and the dc and optical conductivity of SrVO$_3$, a prototypical, moderately correlated metal, within this framework. The degenerate $t_{2g}$ active space of SrVO$_3$, together with its well-established Fermi-liquid behavior, admits an effective-mass description governed by a single quasiparticle weight, yielding a nearly universal picture of the low-frequency, low-temperature regime. Employing a computationally efficient, real-frequency multi-orbital iterative perturbation theory (MO-IPT) impurity solver, we find reasonable agreement with experimental measurements of dc resistivity and optical conductivity across the entire experimentally relevant $(ω, T)$ range within a single, unified scheme. The agreement is shown to not depend on specific interaction parameters provided the quasiparticle weight is kept constant. These results indicate that, in SrVO$_3$, the $\mathbf{k}$-dependence of the self-energy may be weak, and vertex corrections may not dominate the dc and optical transport in this material.

processed.summary:
* Employs a unified **DFT+DMFT (Density Functional Theory + Dynamical Mean-Field Theory)** framework to model spectral and transport properties of correlated metals.
* Implements a computationally efficient **MO-IPT (Multi-Orbital Iterative Perturbation Theory)** impurity solver to calculate real-frequency conductivity.
* Demonstrates that the low-frequency regime of SrVO3 is governed by a **single quasiparticle weight**, simplifying the effective-mass description.
* Validates the approach by matching **dc resistivity and optical conductivity** across a wide range of temperatures and frequencies without dominant vertex corrections.
key_results: Achieved quantitative agreement with experimental dc resistivity and optical conductivity data.
Determined that transport results remain consistent regardless of interaction parameters if quasiparticle weight is constant.
Validated the MO-IPT solver as an efficient real-frequency alternative for multi-orbital systems.
Confirmed that k-dependence of the self-energy is weak in the SrVO3 material model.
Established a nearly universal picture of the low-temperature regime using Fermi-liquid behavior.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate a unified DFT+DMFT framework for accurately modeling transport properties in SrVO3 using efficient MO-IPT solvers.

title: Dynamic neural manifolds for flexible closed-loop control on neuromorphic hardware
summary: In biological circuits, sequential neural activity evolves along dynamic, low-dimensional manifolds to enable flexible behavior. Spiking network models link aspects of this sequential activity to features of manifold geometry through specific circuit mechanisms, making dynamic neural manifolds parameterizable, and thereby offering an explainable framework for neural computation. Extending this framework to neuromorphic engineering, we present an implementation on the SpiNNaker 2 chip for real-time, closed-loop control. By allowing sensory inputs to modulate heterogeneous inhibition, gain, and transient currents, our architecture drives rapid subspace rotations to switch between behaviors, as well as fine-grained trajectory control within them. We validate this via a robotic simulation where an agent uses sensory feedback to dynamically reconfigure its manifold geometry to navigate through a maze. Our results establish dynamic manifolds as a feasible approach for explainable neuromorphic architectures and a substrate for investigating biological neural dynamics.

processed.summary:
- Implements **dynamic neural manifolds** on the **SpiNNaker 2** neuromorphic chip for real-time, closed-loop robotic control.
- Architecture leverages **spiking neural networks** where sensory inputs modulate heterogeneous inhibition, gain, and transient currents to drive **subspace rotations**.
- Provides an **explainable framework** by parameterizing manifold geometry, allowing for direct mapping between circuit mechanisms and behavioral switching.
- Bridges the gap between **biological neural dynamics** and neuromorphic engineering to create energy-efficient, adaptive agents.
key_results: Successful deployment of dynamic manifold controllers on SpiNNaker 2 neuromorphic hardware.
Real-time reconfiguration of manifold geometry based on sensory feedback for behavior switching.
Demonstration of fine-grained trajectory control within specific behavioral subspaces.
Validation of the architecture via a simulated robotic agent navigating a complex maze.
Establishment of a hardware-efficient method for implementing explainable, low-dimensional neural dynamics.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: TU Dresden researchers utilize the SpiNNaker 2 chip to enable real-time, explainable robotic control through dynamic neural manifolds.
